chunk_summary: Guidance on feeding children aged 12–24 months, focusing on family foods, portion sizes, dietary diversity, and limits on certain foods to ensure balanced nutrition and safety.
learning_objectives: Understand appropriate portion sizes and meal frequency for children aged 12–24 months.
Learn strategies to ensure dietary diversity, including food group recommendations.
Identify foods to limit or avoid, such as excessive cow’s milk, sugary drinks, and choking hazards.
Promote self-feeding and mealtime structure to support development and appetite regulation.
key_facts: Children aged 12–24 months should have 3 family meals and 2 planned snacks daily.
Portion sizes are typically three-quarters to one small bowl per meal, adjusted for appetite and growth.
Aim for at least 4 food groups daily: grains/tubers, pulses/nuts, dairy, eggs or flesh foods, and fruits/vegetables.
Limit cow’s milk to 400–500 mL/day to prevent iron deficiency and avoid bottles.
Avoid choking hazards like whole nuts, grapes, and hard raw vegetables unless modified.
Encourage self-feeding with child-sized utensils to support oral-motor development.
reference_quote: From 12–24 months, the child should largely eat family foods modified for safety, while breastfeeding can continue on demand.
document_key_topics: Dietary diversity and food group recommendations
Portion sizes and meal frequency for 12–24 months
Safety and limits on specific foods
